stories.headline: US-IRAN CEASEFIRE TALKS CONTINUE; OIL PRICES SLIDE
stories.body: Oil dropped ~7% on optimism that US and Iran could reach a permanent nuclear deal, with both sides considering fresh negotiations. WTI fell to $92/bbl, Brent to $99/bbl. Markets rose modestly as geopolitical risk eased.

stories.headline: YIELD CURVE STEEPENS AFTER 27-MONTH INVERSION
stories.body: The 10-2 spread reached +54 bps after the longest yield curve inversion in US history. 10Y at 4.29%, 2Y at 3.75%. The normalization signals potential Fed policy shift and easing recession risks, supporting equity valuations.

stories.headline: IMF WARNS ON ELEVATED VALUATIONS; CREDIT SPREADS AT EXTREMES
stories.body: IMF Global Financial Stability Report flags valuations and credit spreads at historically tight levels. EM headline inflation seen stabilizing at 3.2% in 2026, but asset price corrections may test policy capacity in lower-income nations.

stories.headline: RUSSELL 2000 OUTPERFORMS; SMALL CAPS LEAD ROTATION
stories.body: Small caps rallied with Russell 2000 +1.52%, topping S&P 500 +0.40% and Nasdaq +0.93%. Risk-on sentiment, dovish macro, and ceasefire optimism boosted cyclical rotation into beaten-down names.

stories.headline: MICHIGAN CAPS DOMINANT RUN WITH NCAA CHAMPIONSHIP
stories.body: Michigan defeated UConn 79-72 for the title, capping a historic tournament with five 90+ point games—an all-time record. No. 1 seed blew out Arizona 91-73 in semis; UConn beat Illinois 71-62 to reach the final.

stories.headline: PLAY-IN TOURNAMENT TIPS OFF; HORNETS SHOCK WITH 3-POINT BARRAGE
stories.body: SoFi NBA Play-In begins tonight with Heat-Hornets (7:30 ET) and Blazers-Suns (10 ET) on Prime. Rookie Kon Knueppel led league with 273 three-pointers. Hornets' 16.4 made 3s/game at 37.8% clip is second-best in NBA history.

stories.headline: DRAFT APRIL 23; SEAHAWKS TARGET EDGE, RB, CB
stories.body: 2026 NFL Draft begins April 23-25 in Pittsburgh. Seahawks hold 32nd overall; top needs are running back (Kenneth Walker exit, Charbonnet injury), edge (Boye Mafe departure), cornerback (Woolen and Bryant departures). Mock drafts split all three directions.

stories.headline: SPRING GAMES RAMP UP; RECRUITING CLASS RESHUFFLES
stories.body: Spring football in full swing with Clemson, Syracuse, Virginia Tech hosting recruits. USC landed top-ranked recruiting class. Transfer portal continues reshaping rosters; Washington landed 4-star QB Blake Roskopf for 2027 class.

sports.detail: Spring practice ongoing. Four-star QB Blake Roskopf committed (2027 class). Spring game TBD.
Lost to Oregon. Zoom Diallo (13.5 ppg) entered transfer portal. Coach Sprinkle seeking shooters. Hosts USC for senior night.
Pick 32 overall. Top needs: RB (Kenneth Walker exit, Charbonnet injury), edge (Mafe departure), CB (secondary departures).
60-22 record, #1 East seed clinched (April 4 vs 76ers). First 60-win season since 2005-06. Playoffs ahead.
Beat Houston in sweep. Naylor 2 HRs, 5 RBIs. Record 8-9. Next: vs. Angels Saturday 7:10 PM PT at T-Mobile Park.

watchlist: Mariners vs. Angels — Saturday 4/19, 7:10 PM PT at T-Mobile Park
NBA Play-In Tournament — Rounds run through April 21; Pistons in playoffs
2026 NFL Draft — April 23-25, Pittsburgh; Seahawks pick 32 overall
CPI release — Thursday 4/17 at 8:30 AM ET; consensus +0.3% MoM
Fed Chair Powell speaks — Friday 4/18 at Economic Club of NY

deepDive.title: The Yield Curve Steepens: What 27 Months of Inversion Reveals About Recession Timing
content: After the longest yield-curve inversion in US history—27 months of 10-year Treasury yields below 2-year yields—the spread finally turned positive today at +54 basis points. This inflection warrants careful analysis because yield curve inversions are among the most reliable recession predictors in macro, yet timing the actual downturn proves devilishly difficult. The shape of the curve encodes forward expectations about growth, inflation, and Fed policy. A normal, upward-sloping curve reflects a risk premium: lenders demand compensation for locking capital in longer-duration bonds. An inverted curve signals that the market expects the Fed to cut rates—implying economic weakness ahead. This inversion began when the Fed raised the funds rate from near-zero in 2022 to over 5% by mid-2023, crushing short-end yields while longer bonds offered price appreciation protection.

Mechanically, the inversion persisted because the market priced in sustained rate cuts, a judgment vindicated only when growth actually weakened. Yet growth proved more resilient than expected, buoyed by strong labor markets and tech-driven productivity gains. The 10-2 spread bottomed at approximately -150 basis points in mid-2023, then gradually normalized throughout 2024 and into 2026. Why the delay? The lag between inversion and recession typically spans 6–24 months, but the Fed's rate-cut cycle didn't commence as aggressively as markets had priced in. Historical evidence is sobering: every recession since 1955 followed a yield curve inversion. However, the *length* of inversion matters enormously. A brief dip below zero often recovers without recession; a 27-month sustained inversion suggests the economy is repriced for material stress. Investors who hedged recession risk too early in 2022–2024 missed the tech rally that drove S&P 500 gains.

The re-steepening today, driven partly by optimism over US-Iran ceasefire talks and commodity weakness, carries different signals depending on *how* the curve moves. A steepening driven by long rates *falling* (as we're seeing) is less recessionary than one driven by near-term Fed cuts. Watch the 2-year yield; if it falls sharply, markets are pricing imminent rate cuts. A 2Y at 3.75% still signals some policy tightness. Additionally, credit spreads remain near historic tights—investment-grade OAS well below 100 bp—which would typically widen substantially if recession fears resurface. The Fed's inflation fight took two years to bore into the yield curve; now we'll see whether the normalization happens smoothly (soft landing) or precedes actual economic contraction (recession). The market's 27-month warning signal was directionally correct but timing-wise proved uncertain.

For a finance student, the key lesson is this: predictive *direction* (inversion signals weakness ahead) differs from *timing* (actual recession arrival). The curve was right on direction but uncertain on timing. Practical implications: (1) a steepening curve typically accompanies early-cycle improvement, supporting equities; (2) if the 10Y breaks below 4% while 2Y stays firm, that's genuine disinflation signaling and could drive outperformance of long-duration growth names; (3) credit spreads are the canary in the coal mine—today's tightness is fragile. Watch Thursday's CPI, Powell's Friday speech, and the next Fed minutes for clues on whether policymakers see a soft or hard landing ahead.
